perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, United States of America, without giving effect to its principles or rules regarding conflicts of laws, other than such principles directing application of New York law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, United States of America.
perturbation.explanation: Omitting 'without giving effect to its principles or rules regarding conflicts of laws, other than such principles directing application of New York law' creates ambiguity. New York law mandates that conflict of law principles are considered, and omitting this clause could be interpreted as an attempt to bypass that requirement, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires courts to consider conflict of laws principles. By removing the statement that acknowledges conflicts of law principles, the contract suggests that New York law will be applied without regard to conflicts of law, which is a misrepresentation of NY law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 (2022) - Choice of Law. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GOB - General Obligations Article 5 - Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations Title 14 - Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum in Certain Transactions 5-1401 - Choice of Law. Universal Citation: NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. New York may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site
perturbation.justification: The original contract language explicitly acknowledges the application of conflict of law principles under New York law. The changed text omits this acknowledgment, potentially implying that such principles are not to be considered. The Law Explanation states this 'suggests that New York law will be applied without regard to conflicts of law, which is a misrepresentation of NY law.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 (NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401) addresses the choice of law, indicating New York law recognizes and allows parties to agree on choice of law. The Perturbation Explanation notes this omission creates ambiguity regarding the consideration of conflict of law principles, creating a contradiction because it deviates from standard practice. The omission removes an element that ensures compliance with conflict of laws principles, creating a legal loophole, which falls under the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information from the issuer of such securities.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement, and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information,' the clause fails to emphasize the responsibility to inform representatives about insider trading restrictions and to prevent tipping, weakening the preventative measures against illegal activity. This is legally risky, and would open the company to possible penalties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibits the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The SEC has interpreted this to include insider trading and tipping. Omitting the duty to advise representatives about the restrictions weakens the measures taken to comply with insider trading regulations, increasing potential legal exposure for the company.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation for each party to advise its representatives about restrictions imposed by securities laws, which weakens measures against insider trading. The Law Explanation states Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices, including insider trading. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 15 USC 78j, which addresses manipulative and deceptive devices, aligning with the Law Explanation's reference to Section 10(b). Omitting the duty to advise representatives increases legal exposure, creating a contradiction of the law.

perturbation.original_text: Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available to either party hereto, each party hereto shall be entitled to equitable relief by way of injunction or otherwise if the other party or any of its Representatives breach or threaten to breach any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto shall be entitled to equitable relief if the other party or any of its Representatives breach any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available to either party hereto' and 'or threaten to breach' limits the remedies available to the parties. By removing 'without prejudice,' the clause could be interpreted as limiting the parties' recourse solely to equitable relief, excluding other potential remedies at law, like monetary damages. By removing 'or threaten to breach', the parties can only pursue legal action once damages are already inflicted and not to proactively prevent them.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law allows for a range of remedies for breach of contract, including monetary damages and specific performance. By implying that equitable relief is the *only* remedy, the modified contract could be interpreted as an illegal attempt to limit the remedies available to the parties, which contradicts established principles of New York contract law.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available to either party hereto' and 'or threaten to breach' creates a contradiction because it limits the available remedies and the timing of legal action. The original text preserves all available remedies, while the changed text potentially restricts them to only equitable relief. Similarly, the original text allows for action based on a threat of breach, whereas the changed text requires an actual breach. The Law Explanation states that New York law provides a range of remedies for breach of contract and that limiting the remedies available to the parties would contradict established principles of New York contract law. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address this issue, the Law Explanation's argument, combined with the significant limiting effect of the omission, supports the conclusion that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that neither it nor its Representatives nor any of the officers, directors, employees, agents or controlling persons of such Representatives makes any express or implied representation or warranty as to the completeness of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that it makes no express or implied representation or warranty as to the completeness of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'nor its Representatives nor any of the officers, directors, employees, agents or controlling persons of such Representatives', the company limits the scope of the disclaimer. This is legally risky, as the company may still be held liable for the actions of their representatives regarding the completeness of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under agency law, knowledge possessed by an agent is generally imputed to the principal. By removing the disclaimer concerning the representations of representatives, the contract increases the risk that a party could be held liable for misrepresentations made by its agents, even if the party itself did not make those representations. This contradicts the principles of agency law regarding the imputation of knowledge and liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly disclaims representations or warranties made by the party's representatives concerning the completeness of confidential information. The modified text removes this disclaimer for representatives, which, according to agency law, can create a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party.' By removing the disclaimer, the principal is potentially liable for the agent's representations, thus contradicting the initial intent to exclude liability for representatives' actions and creating an omission of essential information related to liability, as the company is now potentially liable for their representatives actions.
